turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court in case 21-857, Jones versus Hendrix.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on the writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for The Eighth Circuit. The question presented arises at the intersection of two statutes, governing federal prisoners collateral attacks on their sentences. Since 1948, Congress has required a federal prisoner seeking collateral review to file a motion in the sentencing court under 28 USC 2255 in lieu of filing a habeas corpus petition wherever he happens to be in prison. However, in the provision known as the Saving Clause, Congress provided that a prisoner could still file a habeas petition if Section 2255 would be “inadequate or ineffective” to test the legality of his detention. Later, Congress passed the Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act or AEDPA. AEDPA now bars alls second or successive Section 2255 motions with exceptions for claims based on new evidence or new rules of constitutional law. But AEDPA has no exception for claims raising new statutory interpretations. In the case before us, petitioner argues that this omission makes Section 2255 in adequate or ineffective under the Saving Clause, thereby permitting a habeas petition if a prisoner wishes to pursue a second or successive collateral attack based on an intervening decision of this Court reinterpreting a criminal statute. The district court and the Eighth Circuit rejected that argument and dismissed petitioner's habeas application. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we affirm. We hold that the Saving Clause does not authorize circumventing AEDPA's second or successive restrictions. Before AEDPA, courts generally interpreted Saving Clause to cover unusual circumstances in which a prisoner could not file a Section 2255 motion. AEDPA then limited second or a successive motions to two circumstances, which do not include new statutory interpretations. Petitioner's argument that this omission makes Section 2255 inadequate or ineffective has no basis in the text, history, or structure of Section 2255. Petitioners' reading of the Saving Clause also is not necessary to avoid any constitutional doubts about Section 2255 as amended. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Eight Circuit. Justice Sotomayor and Justice Kagan have filed a joint dissenting opinion. Justice Jackson has also filed a dissenting opinion.
